_comment: 40 maturity questions, 5 per dimension. Each question maps answer options to DAMA-DMBOK maturity levels 1-5. See content/schemas/questions.schema.json for the full schema.

questions.question: Is there a named person accountable for each major data domain in your organisation?
questions.help_text: A data domain is a distinct subject area of data — for example, customer data, financial data, HR data, or operational data.

options.label: No — nobody is formally responsible for any data domain. Ownership is unclear.
Informally — a few people are known to look after certain data, but it is not documented or recognised as a role.
Partially — named owners exist for our most important data domains, with documented responsibilities.
Broadly — most domains have named owners within a defined accountability framework, with regular check-ins.
Fully — every domain has a named owner with active stewardship duties, regular reviews, and clear escalation paths.

questions.question: Does your organisation have documented data policies covering access, quality, retention, and acceptable use?
questions.help_text: Policies don't need to be a single document — separate policies for each topic are fine. The question is whether they exist and people know where to find them.

options.label: No documented policies exist. Rules are communicated informally if at all.
Some policies exist (often written for a compliance deadline) but they are hard to find and not widely read.
Core policies are documented, accessible, and applied consistently across our main systems and teams.
Policies are comprehensive, regularly reviewed on a set schedule, and ownership of each policy is clearly assigned.
Policies are version-controlled, enforced through tooling where possible, and updated based on incidents and regulatory changes.

questions.question: Does your organisation have a data governance body — such as a committee, council, or working group — with real decision-making authority?
questions.help_text: This could be a formal Data Governance Board or an informal cross-functional group. The key question is whether it can make and enforce decisions about data.

options.label: No governance body exists. Data decisions are made ad hoc by whoever is available.
There are occasional cross-team meetings about data, but no standing forum with a defined remit.
A governance body exists with a defined remit, meets regularly, and has resolved at least some real data disputes.
The governance body has documented authority, a published meeting schedule, minutes, and an active backlog of decisions.
The governance body drives strategic data direction, reviews metrics, owns the policy framework, and reports to senior leadership.

questions.question: Does your organisation classify data by sensitivity — for example, distinguishing between public, internal, confidential, and sensitive personal data?
questions.help_text: A classification scheme means data assets are labelled or tagged with their sensitivity level, not just that the categories exist in a policy document.

options.label: No classification scheme. All data is treated the same regardless of sensitivity.
Sensitivity categories exist on paper but data is not consistently labelled or tagged in practice.
A classification scheme is in place and applied consistently to our most sensitive and highest-risk data assets.
Classification is applied broadly across the estate and is integrated with access control — permissions reflect classification level.
Classification is automated at ingestion, enforced by tooling, and linked to retention, masking, and audit policies.

questions.question: How does your organisation measure and track compliance with its data governance policies?
questions.help_text: This is about whether you know whether policies are being followed — not just whether the policies exist.

options.label: We do not measure compliance. Policy adherence depends on individuals being aware of and choosing to follow the rules.
Compliance is checked occasionally, usually before an audit or in response to an incident.
Key compliance indicators are tracked and reported at least quarterly, with a process for raising and resolving breaches.
Compliance metrics are part of regular governance reporting. Non-compliance triggers a formal triage and resolution process.
Policy compliance is continuously monitored through automated tooling. Exceptions are detected and escalated in near real-time.

questions.question: Can people in your organisation find out what data exists without asking a colleague?
questions.help_text: Think about how a new analyst would discover what datasets are available. Could they do it independently, or do they rely on asking someone who already knows?

options.label: No — discovery depends entirely on knowing the right person to ask. There is no central inventory.
Partially — some teams maintain their own lists or wikis, but there is no shared starting point and coverage is patchy.
Mostly — a catalogue or inventory covers our core datasets and most people know where to look.
Yes — a maintained catalogue covers most of the estate and is the recognised first stop for data discovery.
Fully — the catalogue is comprehensive, kept current automatically, and actively used by both technical and non-technical staff.

questions.question: Does your organisation maintain a business glossary — a shared, agreed definition of key business terms and metrics?
questions.help_text: A business glossary defines terms like 'active customer', 'revenue', or 'incident' so that everyone means the same thing. It is separate from technical documentation.

options.label: No — definitions exist only in people's heads or vary between teams. The same term means different things in different places.
Informally — some teams have agreed definitions internally, but there is no shared glossary and cross-team alignment is rare.
Partially — a business glossary exists covering our most important terms and metrics, though it is incomplete and not always kept up to date.
Broadly — the glossary covers most key terms, is linked to physical datasets in the catalogue, and is reviewed regularly.
Comprehensively — the glossary is actively maintained, widely used, linked to metric definitions in our semantic layer, and governs how KPIs are defined.

questions.question: Can you trace where a piece of data came from and how it has been transformed before reaching a report or dashboard?
questions.help_text: This is called data lineage. For example: can you follow customer revenue from its source system through your pipelines to the finance dashboard?

options.label: No — lineage is unknown. When a number looks wrong, we cannot easily trace where it came from.
Partially — lineage is documented informally (e.g. in a diagram or a README) for some pipelines, but not systematically.
For key pipelines — lineage is captured and documented for our most critical data flows, making it possible to trace issues.
Broadly — lineage is tracked automatically for most pipelines and is visible in our catalogue or orchestration tool.
End-to-end — full column-level lineage is captured automatically across all pipelines, from source to dashboard, and is always current.

questions.question: How complete and useful is the documentation attached to your datasets — including owner, description, refresh frequency, and known issues?
questions.help_text: Think about the metadata quality of a typical important dataset, not just your best-documented one.

options.label: Datasets have little or no documentation. A new user has no way of knowing what a dataset contains or how to use it.
Some datasets have basic descriptions, but coverage is inconsistent and documentation quickly goes out of date.
Core datasets have documented owners, descriptions, and refresh cadences. Known quality issues are noted for the most critical datasets.
Most datasets have rich documentation. Quality is measured and catalogue entries are kept current through a defined maintenance process.
Documentation is a first-class deliverable. Pipeline automation populates metadata on publish. Entry quality is measured and feeds team objectives.

questions.question: Do business users — not just data engineers — actively contribute to or use your metadata catalogue?
questions.help_text: A catalogue maintained only by the data team tends to drift from business reality. This question is about whether the business has a voice in defining and describing data.

options.label: No catalogue exists, or only the data team can access it. Business users are not involved.
The catalogue is technically available to all but in practice only used by technical staff. Business users do not contribute.
Some business users are aware of the catalogue and occasionally update descriptions or flag issues.
Business users regularly use the catalogue for discovery and contribute domain knowledge — descriptions, glossary terms, and usage notes.
The catalogue is a shared workspace. Business owners curate their domain's assets. Usage analytics show broad, active adoption across the organisation.

questions.question: How are data quality problems typically discovered in your organisation?
questions.help_text: Think about who spots quality issues and at what point in the process — before data is used, or after someone notices something is wrong.

options.label: By end users noticing something looks wrong — a report breaks, a figure seems implausible, or a complaint arrives.
By analysts running manual spot-checks before key reports are published, but not systematically or for all data.
By automated checks that run on our most critical datasets and alert the data team when thresholds are breached.
By a monitoring layer that continuously checks quality across most of the estate and routes alerts to the right owners.
By anomaly detection that surfaces issues proactively — often before any downstream consumer has been affected.

questions.question: Does your organisation have defined quality dimensions — such as completeness, accuracy, timeliness, and consistency — applied to its data?
questions.help_text: Quality dimensions are the specific properties you measure. Without defining them, 'good quality' means different things to different people.

options.label: No defined quality dimensions. Quality is assessed informally based on whether data 'looks right'.
Some teams have implicit quality expectations but they are not formally defined, documented, or consistently applied.
A quality framework with defined dimensions is in place and applied to our most critical datasets with measurable thresholds.
Quality dimensions are defined across most of the estate, with per-dataset thresholds, owners, and a formal breach process.
Quality rules are codified as tests in our pipeline tooling, applied at every stage, and continuously refined based on incident data.

questions.question: When a data quality incident occurs, does your organisation have a defined process for triaging, fixing, and learning from it?
questions.help_text: A quality incident is any case where bad data reached a consumer — a wrong report, a broken dashboard, or incorrect data in a downstream system.

options.label: No process. Whoever notices the problem fixes it as best they can. The same issues recur regularly.
The data team investigates when issues are reported, but there is no standard triage process and root causes are rarely documented.
A defined incident process exists — issues are logged, triaged, assigned, and resolved within agreed timeframes for critical data.
Incidents are tracked over time. Root cause analysis is performed for recurring issues. Quality debt is maintained as a visible backlog.
Incident retrospectives feed directly into pipeline improvements and test coverage. The defect rate demonstrably decreases over time.

questions.question: Are data quality metrics reported to business stakeholders — not just tracked internally by the data team?
questions.help_text: This is about whether quality is treated as a shared concern, or as something the data team manages in private.

options.label: No quality metrics exist. Stakeholders have no visibility of data quality unless they raise an issue themselves.
The data team tracks some quality stats internally but does not share them with business stakeholders in any regular way.
Key quality metrics are reported to relevant stakeholders at least monthly, with context on trends and outstanding issues.
Quality scorecards are part of regular data reporting. SLAs (service level agreements) for critical datasets are agreed and tracked.
Quality metrics are embedded in data product dashboards. Stakeholders can self-serve quality information. SLA performance is publicly visible within the organisation.

questions.question: Are data quality checks embedded as automated tests in your data pipelines — running on every load, not just on demand?
questions.help_text: For example, using tools like dbt tests, Great Expectations, or Soda Core to validate data as it flows through the pipeline rather than checking it manually afterwards.

options.label: No automated tests. Quality is checked manually, if at all, after data has already been loaded.
Some ad hoc SQL checks or scripts exist but they are not part of the pipeline and must be run manually.
Automated tests run on our most critical pipelines and block a load from completing if key checks fail.
Automated tests are standard practice across most pipelines. Test results are logged and visible in a monitoring dashboard.
Every pipeline stage has automated quality gates. Test coverage is measured and tracked. Failed tests trigger automated alerts and rollback where possible.
